perturbation.original_text: Parties hereby acknowledge, covenant and agree that they are aware that United States securities laws may prohibit any person who has material, non-public information about a company (including a Party) from purchasing or selling, directly or indirectly, securities of a company (including the Parties), or from communicating such information to any other person under circumstances in which it is reasonably foreseeable that such person is likely to purchase or sell such securities.
perturbation.changed_text: All Confidential Information shall be returned to the disclosing Parties promptly upon written request or, at the election of the disclosing Party, the Party that received the Confidential Information shall certify said information has been destroyed and is no longer useable in any format. Parties hereby acknowledge, covenant and agree that they are aware that United States securities laws may prohibit any person who has material, non-public information about a company (including a Party) from purchasing or selling, directly or indirectly, securities of a company (including the Parties), or from communicating such information to any other person under circumstances in which it is reasonably foreseeable that such person is likely to purchase or sell such securities.
perturbation.explanation: Moving the acknowledgement of securities laws to the 'Return of Confidential Information' section buries it where it's less likely to be noticed in relation to trading activities, thus making it difficult to enforce.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Securities Exchange Act of 1934 - Section 10(b) and Rule 10b-5
perturbation.law_explanation: Rule 10b-5 prohibits the use of any manipulative or deceptive device or contrivance in connection with the purchase or sale of any security. By burying the disclosure of securities law awareness in the return of confidential information section, the agreement reduces emphasis on this critical regulation for individuals possessing non-public information, which reduces clarity around enforcement.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 17 CFR § 240.10b-5 - Employment of manipulative and deceptive devices. | Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (e-CFR) | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you 17 CFR § 240.10b-5 - Employment of manipulative and deceptive devices. CFR prev | next § 240.10b-5 Employment of manipulative and deceptive devices. It shall be unlawful for any person, directly or indirectly, by the use of any means or instrumentality of interstate commerce, or of the mails or of any facility of any national securities exchange , (a) To employ any device, scheme, or artifice to defraud, (b) To make any untrue statement of a material fact or to omit to state a material fact necessary in order to make the statements made, in the light of the circumstances under which they were made, not misleading, or (c) To engage in any act, practice, or course of business which operates or would operate as a fraud or deceit upon any person, in connection with the purchase or sale of any security . (Sec. 10; 48 Stat. 891 ; 15 U.S.C. 78j ) [ 13 FR 8183 , Dec. 22, 1948, as amended at 16 FR 7928 , Aug. 11, 1951] CFR Toolbox Law about... perturbation.justification: The modified text buries the acknowledgement of securities laws within the 'Return of Confidential Information' section, which constitutes a structural flaw. According to the perturbation definition, a structural flaw leads to a legal contradiction when a required legal disclosure is placed in an irrelevant section, making it difficult for parties to locate or interpret. The Law Explanation supports this by stating that this placement 'reduces emphasis' on the securities law, reducing clarity around enforcement. Scraped Law Snippet 1, particularly section (c), prohibits any act that operates as a fraud or deceit in connection with the purchase or sale of any security. By obscuring the acknowledgement of securities laws, the modified text weakens the preventative effect of informing individuals about insider trading prohibitions, thus increasing the risk of violating Rule 10b-5.
